ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Soups and broths) is the closest parent category. No specific code exists for Korean seaweed soup in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Korean seaweed soup or miyeokguk.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Korean seaweed soup is approximately 300 g, typically served as a side dish or light main course alongside steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Miyeokguk (Seaweed Soup) — National Institute of Agricultural Sciences (NAAS) Korean Food Composition Database, Item Code 10-0-0001
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Agricultural Sciences, Rural Development Administration, Republic of Korea (NAAS/RDA) — Korean Food Composition Database (KFCD)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced from the Korean Food Composition Database (KFCD) entry for miyeokguk (seaweed soup, beef-based). The KFCD is the authoritative national food composition database for Korean foods, published by the National Institute of Agricultural Sciences under the Rural Development Administration of the Republic of Korea. This is the primary approved source for Korean dishes under the FAO/INFOODS regional source hierarchy. Per-100g values reflect the standard prepared soup as consumed.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of vegetable and seaweed-based soups — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate broth-type soups; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for miyeokguk in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 15 based on analogy with low-carbohydrate vegetable and seaweed-based broths. The soup contains approximately 1.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g (predominantly from seaweed polysaccharides with very low glycemic impact), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 0.5 per 300 g serving. This estimate is consistent with the low-GI classification of seaweed-containing foods documented in peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). GI category is low (≤55).

hypertension.guidance: Miyeokguk contains approximately 280 mg sodium per 100 g (840 mg per standard 300 g bowl), which represents a moderate sodium load; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-salt preparation to keep daily sodium within the recommended 2000 mg limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Miyeokguk has a very low carbohydrate content of approximately 1.2 g per 100 g and an estimated glycemic index of 15, making it a suitable soup choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added sugar or high-GI accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, beneficial seaweed bioactives
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Miyeokguk is low in saturated fat (approximately 0.2 g per 100 g) and contains wakame seaweed, which provides fucoidan and other bioactive compounds associated with anti-inflammatory and lipid-modulating effects; however, the moderate sodium content warrants attention in individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Miyeokguk (미역국), commonly known as Korean seaweed soup, is a traditional Korean soup prepared by simmering rehydrated dried wakame seaweed (miyeok) in a light broth typically made with beef, clams, or anchovies, seasoned with sesame oil, garlic, and soy sauce. The soup has a mild, slightly oceanic flavour with a silky, tender texture from the rehydrated seaweed. It is deeply embedded in Korean culture as the traditional birthday soup, consumed by mothers after childbirth for postpartum recovery due to its iodine and calcium content, and eaten by Koreans on their birthdays as a cultural tradition. Nutritionally, miyeokguk is a very low-calorie, low-carbohydrate soup providing approximately 15 kcal per 100 g, with modest protein (1.4 g), minimal fat (0.6 g), and a low glycemic index estimated at 15. It is a good source of iodine, calcium (38 mg per 100 g), and vitamin B12 (0.3 mcg per 100 g) from the seaweed and animal protein components. The sodium content of approximately 280 mg per 100 g is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Wakame seaweed contains fucoidan, a sulfated polysaccharide with documented anti-inflammatory and potential lipid-lowering properties. The soup is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycemic impact. In Singapore, miyeokguk is served at Korean restaurants as a side dish or light main, often accompanying bibimbap or Korean rice bowl sets.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Korean seaweed soup?
What is birthday soup in Korean culture?
Is seaweed soup high in sodium?
What are the health benefits of miyeokguk?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from the Korean Food Composition Database (KFCD), which is the authoritative national database for Korean foods and aligns with FAO/INFOODS regional source policy. The GI value is an analogous estimate based on the low-carbohydrate, seaweed-based broth profile, as no direct GI measurement for miyeokguk exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the analogous GI estimation.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the Korean Food Composition Database entry for miyeokguk. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the Korean Food Composition Database entry for miyeokguk. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the Korean Food Composition Database entry for miyeokguk. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero per KFCD source — naturally zero in this minimally processed soup. The GI of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement exists for miyeokguk in the University of Sydney GI Database. The sodium value of 280 mg per 100 g reflects a standard beef-based preparation; sodium may vary significantly depending on the amount of soy sauce and salt added during cooking. The restaurant record from field survey data (Mincheng Bibimbap, Funan) at SGD 0.80 likely represents a side-dish portion rather than a full bowl. Multilingual coverage for Malay, Tamil, and Hindi is not applicable for this Korean dish and has been left null in local_names per the cuisine-specific multilingual rules; Korean is the mandatory local-language requirement for Korean cuisine.
